retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The five histologic subtypes encountered in medulloblastoma include: classic desmoplastic-nodular (D/N) large-cell anaplastic (LC/A) melanotic medullomyoblastoma

retrieved_docs.title: Myxopapillary Ependymoma(Archived) -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations that are typically requested for patients with this condition include the following: Neurosurgeon Neurologist Radiotherapist Radiologist Pathologist

retrieved_docs.title: Neuronal Brain Tumors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The purely neuronal group includes gangliocytoma, multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT), dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease), central neurocytoma, extraventricular neurocytoma, and cerebellar liponeurocytoma. The mixed glioneuronal group includes ganglioglioma, desmoplastic infantile ganglioglioma/desmoplastic infantile astrocytoma (DIG/DIA), dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET), diffuse glioneuronal tumor with oligodendroglioma-like features and nuclear clusters (DGONC), papillary glioneuronal tumor (PGT), rosette-forming glioneuronal tumor (RGNT), myxoid glioneuronal tumor (MGT), and diffuse leptomeningeal glioneuronal tumor (DLGNT). MVNT, DGONC, and MGT are newly classified entities in the WHO CNS tumor classification schema.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Brain Tumors -- Evaluation -- Embryonal Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Embryonal tumors in this age group include ATRT, medulloblastoma, and embryonal tumor with multilayered rosettes (ETMR), C19MC-altered. MRI characteristics of ATRT include a heterogeneous mass that may be partially cystic and demonstrate areas of necrosis, intralesional hemorrhage, or mineralization. On both T1- and T2-weighted images, they are typically isointense to hypointense with heterogeneous contrast enhancement. Due to the high cellularity, the solid portion of the tumor often shows restricted diffusion. [13] Because of the propensity for drop metastasis, diagnostic evaluation of the entire spinal axis should ideally occur preoperatively to help distinguish between postoperative blood products within the CSF.

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A study investigating previously histological supratentorial PNET, which reanalyzed them using molecular parameters, found that the diagnosis changed in 71%. [12] A supratentorial lesion has an increased probability of being a high-grade glioma or ependymoma if confused with a supratentorial PNET. DNA methylation profile defines the diagnosis. [14] In infratentorial lesions, medulloblastoma and ependymoma have to be excluded first. They studied brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) characteristics of embryonal tumors, which distinguished them from other molecular diagnosed tumors and found the embryonal tumors have similar edema changes, definition of margins, and size as the non-embryonal tumors and are thus radiologically indistinguishable.

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: ATRT usually shows a loss of SMARCB1 (INI1) protein immunoreactivity. They have three groups (ATRT-TYR frequent at infratentorial regions in very young children, ATRTMYC prevalent at the supratentorial area in older children, ATRT-SHH at both ages). [6] They can be positive for EMA and vimentin.

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In classic medulloblastoma, sheets of small round cells, possessing a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, are noted. They have a high invasive tendency and possess occasional neuroblastic differentiation. The classic type constitutes approximately 70% of medulloblastomas. Nodules of tumor cells displaying neurocytic differentiation on a collagen-rich matrix characterize the desmoplastic variant; these tumors are less aggressive than the classic variant and account for 15% of medulloblastomas. Large-cell anaplastic medulloblastomas, as the designation suggests, demonstrate features of anaplasia. These features are large tumor cells with abundant cytoplasm, pleomorphic nuclei, and prominent nucleoli. These tumors are typically located in the cerebellar vermis and are highly aggressive, demonstrating high mitotic and apoptotic activity with large areas of necrosis. Consequently, the prognosis is especially poor with short survival times after diagnosis. They constitute approximately 10% of medulloblastomas. [18] The last two variants are rare and make up the remaining 5% of medulloblastomas. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, the World Health Organization published a revised classification of central nervous system tumors using molecular parameters. In this classification, some tumors previously recognized in the 2007 classification had been renamed or eliminated. The primitive neuroectodermal tumor is no longer recognized as such and those tumors are now under the classification of embryonal tumors. Medulloblastomas are embryonal tumors but have their own genetically (molecular) and histologically defined groups. The other embryonal tumors are classified depending if they have on chromosome 19 an amplification of the C19MC region (19q13.42). Those embryonal tumors that no amplification of the C19MC, no alterations of INI1 or BRG1, or no rosettes are identified, are called embryonal tumor, NOS (WHO 9473/3). This activity reviews the evaluation, and management of embryonal tumors, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Those embryonal tumors that no amplification of the C19MC, no alterations of INI1 or BRG1, or no rosettes are identified, are called embryonal tumor, NOS (WHO 9473/3). At present, there are no molecular or genetic alterations associated with them. These embryonal tumors have to be well investigated. This is an exclusion diagnosis. They have to be differentiated from supratentorial ependymoma, high-grade glioma, embryonal tumor with multilayered rosettes, and ATRT based on their specific genetic alterations. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultations may be required from neuropathologists, pediatricians, neurosurgeons, hematologist-oncologists, radiation oncologists, and endocrinologists.

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Using a combination of molecular profiling and histology, the World Health Organization (WHO) presently divides medulloblastoma into five subtypes [37] : WNT-activated with classic histology SHH-activated (wild-type TP53), with classic, desmoplastic/nodular, or large-cell/anaplastic histology SHH-activated (mutant TP53), with classic or large-cell/anaplastic histology non-WNT/non-SHH, group 3, with classic or large-cell anaplastic histology non-WNT/non-SHH, group 4, with classic or large-cell/anaplastic histology

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: ATRT: Usually found in patients under the age of two. Ependymomas: Usually have no restriction. High-grade gliomas: The majority are indistinguishable, and diagnosis is based on molecular characteristics. Some can have vascular endothelial proliferation and pseudopalisading necrosis. Medulloblastomas: Have their own genetic/molecular defined groups. Medulloepitheliomas: Usually found in very young patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Choroid plexus carcinoma Ependymoma Teratoma

retrieved_docs.title: Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: CNS neuroblastoma with FOXR2 activation is highly cellular with small cells with hyperchromatic nuclei surrounded by a clear halo. Areas of neuropil, neurocytic cells, or ganglion cells are seen. Vascular pseudorosettes, nuclear palisades, and Homer Wright rosettes are commonly found. It can have microvascular proliferation.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Histopathology -- Microscopic Features
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoblastomas often exhibit characteristic rosette formations, which represent the tumor cells' attempts at differentiation. The most characteristic type is the Flexner-Wintersteiner rosette, in which tumor cells are arranged in a circular fashion around a central lumen, mimicking the photoreceptor layer of the retina. These rosettes reflect photoreceptor differentiation. Homer Wright rosettes are also found, where tumor cells form a circular pattern around a central core of fibrillary material. These rosettes are seen in neuroectodermal tumors and indicate primitive neural differentiation. Fluerette patterns are characterized by loosely formed clusters of cells resembling photoreceptors, which suggest a higher degree of differentiation. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: A CT scan of the head that demonstrates a periventricular cyst is classified as a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية). This imaging finding indicates the presence of a cystic lesion located near the brain’s ventricular system, and it is recognized within medical terminology as a type of intracranial cyst. Identifying the lesion as a brain cyst guides further diagnostic evaluation and informs appropriate clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

Together, these relationships demonstrate how the classification integrates diverse malignant neoplasms, connects specific tumor subtypes to broader malignant categories, identifies anatomical sites of disease extension, and relates morphological descriptors and clinical findings across organ systems, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1082
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, diagnostic observations and pathological findings related to the choroidea are specifically localized to the site termed “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating a genetically related placental structure. Precise identification of choroidal involvement at this site is essential for accurate diagnosis, management, and prognostication of the diverse ocular diseases that arise within the choroidea.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.content: for oxygen support in these conditions. Ocular diagnostic procedures are organized under uncategorized histopathology procedures, a parent category that also includes anatomy, indicating that eye histopathology forms part of a broader anatomical and pathological assessment framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: The CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION OF NEOPLASMS OF THE MUSCULOSKELETAL SYSTEM AND SOFT TISSUES encompasses specific subcategories for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head and for metastatic malignant neoplasm to the supraclavicular region. By defining these child categories, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing patterns of metastasis from musculoskeletal and soft‑tissue tumors to distinct anatomical sites, which is essential for accurate staging, targeted diagnostic work‑up, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2206
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the brain and nervous system is categorized as a malignant brain tumor (ورم الدماغ الخبيث), indicating that neoplasms affecting the brain and nervous system fall under the broader classification of malignant brain tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of temporal bone, indicating that cancerous cells have spread to the temporal bone and are diagnosed as a malignant tumor of that anatomical site. This relationship underscores that the metastatic lesion is considered a specific type of malignant neoplasm affecting the temporal bone, which is relevant for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.
